The '''Catalans Dragons''' ([[French language|French]]: ''Dragons Catalans'', [[Catalan language|Catalan]]: ''Dracs Catalans'') are a professional [[rugby league]] club from [[Perpignan]], [[Pyrénées-Orientales]] department, France. Despite being based in France, the club competes in [[Super League]], the top tier of the [[British rugby league system|British rugby league]].

Formed after the merger of [[XIII Catalan]] and [[AS Saint-Estève]], they have won one [[French Rugby League Championship|French Championship]] and two [[Lord Derby Cup]]s. In Britain they have never won the [[List of British rugby league champions|League Championship]] but have won the [[Challenge Cup]] once.

Catalans play home games at [[Stade Gilbert Brutus]]. Their traditional home colours are white red and gold.

== History ==
===2000–2005: Formation in Elite 1===
The club was founded in 2000 after the merger of two teams in the area of [[Perpignan]], [[XIII Catalan]] and [[AS Saint-Estève]]. The merged team took the name Union Treiziste Catalane, often abbreviated to UTC.

XIII Catalan were founded in 1935 and thus were founding members of the [[French Championship]]. During their run, they won 11 French Championships and 11 [[Lord Derby Cup]]s. [[AS Saint-Estève]] were founded in 1965. They won six championships and four Lord Derby Cups. There were two other clubs in the twelve-team competition in Pyrénées-Orientales: [[Pia XIII]] and Saint-Cyprien. In 2002 Saint-Cyprien joined the merged UTC side. UTC won the 2005 French Championship and the 2004 and 2005 [[Lord Derby Cup]]s.

===2006–2007: Transfer to Super League and Challenge Cup final===
[[File:LesCatalansdragonslogo.png|left|thumb|200px|Catalans Dragons logo used in 2006 and 2007.]]

In 2006, UTC transferred from the [[French rugby league system|French]] to [[British rugby league system]], joining the [[Super League XI]] following a successful application in 2005.<ref name="MEN2005">{{cite news |title=French let in |url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/other-sport/french-let-in-1110896 |access-date=27 September 2025 |work=Manchester Evening News |date=2005}}</ref> They were selected ahead of [[Toulouse Olympique]] and [[Villeneuve Leopards]].{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} After having planned to be called Perpignan, the club was named Catalans Dragons to represent the whole [[Catalonia]] region, with matches played in [[Carcassonne]] and [[Narbonne]], as well as across the border in [[Figueres]] in Spain{{dubious|date=September 2025}} while expansion work at the [[Stade Gilbert Brutus]] took place.<ref name="BBC250505">{{cite news |title=UTC to become Les Catalans in '06 |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/super_league/3640265.stm |access-date=27 September 2025 |work=BBC Sport |date=25 May 2005}}</ref> while a new club, [[Saint-Estève XIII Catalan]], was formed in the French Championship to serve as a reserve team for the club.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} The club set a target for 75% of the players be qualified to play for [[France national rugby league team|France]].<ref name="BBC250505" />

The Catalans are the second French side to play in the Super League. The first, [[Paris Saint-Germain Rugby League|Paris Saint-Germain]], lasted only two seasons. Both [[rugby football|rugby]] codes have their stronghold in the southwest of France, with the north favouring [[association football]].

To ensure that the Catalans had the best French players available to them, the French rugby league decided to let them sign players from other French clubs without paying a transfer fee.{{citation needed|date=September 2025}} The RFL also made them exempt from relegation for their first three years in [[Super League]].<ref name="MEN2005" />

Many{{who|date=April 2019}} at the time believe the Catalans would be joined by other French clubs,{{citation needed|date=April 2019}} but the idea of expanding into France had critics.{{who|date=January 2024}} 

The Catalans won their first ever Super League match 38–30 against [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] on 11 February 2006, at [[Stade Aimé Giral]]. The club encountered difficulties in their first season in the Super League. Many of less experienced French players suffered from tiredness towards the end of an injury-marred campaign. A particular loss included captain Stacey Jones, who missed much of the season with a broken arm. The team finished bottom of the table, but the three-year exemption from relegation kept them in the Super League.

[[File:St Helens Catalans.jpg|thumb|right|Catalans Dragons during their first [[2007 Challenge Cup Final|Challenge Cup Final in 2007]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].]]

The year 2007 saw a strong recruitment by new coach Mick Potter with a string of high-profile signings from Australia, including Clint Greenshields, Casey McGuire, Jason Croker and Aaron Gorrell, all seasoned NRL campaigners. Gorrell, a goalkicking hooker, impressed in the first month but sustained a bad knee injury in February's win over [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] and missed the rest of the season. On 10 March 2007, it was announced that Newcastle Knights hooker [[Luke Quigley]] would cover Gorrell's absence for the remainder of the campaign, but a number of players sustained injuries throughout the campaign.

On 29 July 2007, the Catalans became the first [[France|French]] side and first non-British side to reach the final of the [[Challenge Cup]] after beating [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] 37–24 in the semi-final. The Catalans lost the [[2007 Challenge Cup Final]] against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at [[Wembley Stadium]] on 25 August 2007. They finished the [[Super League XII|2007 season]]  in tenth place.

===2008–2017: Improvement in league results===

[[File:DragonsCatalans2009.jpg|thumb|left|Catalans Dragons playing at Gilbert Brutus in 2009]]

In [[Super League XIII|2008]], the Catalans secured their first playoff berth by finishing third in the league. They thrashed [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] 46–8 in their first-ever playoff match on 13 September in Perpignan, but 20 September saw [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] end up victorious in what had been a close game in the second half of their elimination final, with Wigan winning 50–26.

Coach Mick Potter left the Dragons at the end of the 2008 season to replace Daniel Anderson at St Helens.

In 2009, they were involved in two historic milestones for the sport of rugby league in Europe. During their match away to the [[Wales|Welsh]] club [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] on 23 May, the two clubs played the first Super League match to not feature an English team.<ref name="nonAnglo">{{cite web|url=http://www.rleague.com/db/article.php?id=33325|title=Super League's First Ever International (with no English)|publisher=www.rleague.com (The World of Rugby League)|access-date=2009-06-20|last=O'Neill|first=Matthew|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090525190742/http://www.rleague.com/db/article.php?id=33325|archive-date=2009-05-25}}</ref> History was also created on 20 June, when the club played in the first Super League game to be played in Spain, at [[Barcelona]]'s [[Estadi Olímpic Lluís Companys]], the venue for the [[1992 Summer Olympics]], against [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]]. The Dragons led 10–6 at halftime, but Warrington finished as the winners 12–24.<ref name="BarcaBBCreport">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8104747.stm|title=Catalans Dragons 12–24 Warrington|publisher=news.bbc.co.uk (BBC Sport)|access-date=2009-06-20| date=20 June 2009}}</ref> The purpose of the latter fixture was to promote the sport in [[Catalonia]], with around 1000 tickets being sold in the local area, and the game was televised on the Catalan channel [[El 33]].<ref name="Barcelona">{{cite web |url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=14420 |title=Dragons pleased with Barcelona ticket sales |publisher=www.superleague.co.uk (The [[Rugby Football League|RFL]]) |access-date=2009-06-17 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120405033054/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=14420 |archive-date=2012-04-05 }}</ref> Immediately after the game, Walters commented that the event in Spain could become an annual one,<ref name="BarcaBBCreport" /> apparently complementing comments made by the club's general manager about using a new [[LGV Perpignan-Figueres|high-speed link]] between Perpignan and Spain, supposed to start running within two years.<ref name="Barcelona" />

[[File:Dragons catalans-Wigan (4 juin 2011) -supporters catalans II.jpg|thumb|right|Catalans Dragons supporters during a home game against [[Wigan Warriors]] in 2011]]

In 2016 Catalans Dragons celebrated ten years in the Super League competition. After a recruitment drive, which saw a number of transfers from the NRL, expectations were high. After opening defeats to Wigan and Hull FC, the Dragons edged Leeds 32–28 in round three and then took seven wins from their next eight matches. Following their 42–32 win over Salford at the end of April, Laurent Frayssinous' side sat second in the table, level on points with leaders Warrington. However, a series of injury setbacks to key players in the second half of the season saw them drop down the table. Catalans lost their final six regular season games which meant they headed into the Super 8s two points adrift of fourth-placed St Helens. Five defeats from seven saw them finish the season in sixth place and miss out on a play-off spot. After a difficult two years at Huddersfield, Jodie Broughton's move to the south of France saw him kick-start his career. The winger scored 19 tries in 2016 including four in the Dragons' 30–12 win over St Helens in April. Todd Carney was released after two seasons at Catalans.<ref>[http://www.rugby-league.com/article/39016/season-review-catalans-dragons#sthash.ULwfokkn.dpuf Season Review: Catalans Dragons], 3 December 2016</ref>

===2018–present: Establishment as a top club===
On 25 August 2018, Catalans Dragons won their first British Rugby League trophy, after defeating [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] 20–14 in the [[2018 Challenge Cup|2018 Challenge Cup Final]], in the process becoming the first non-English team to win the competition.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/45260134|title=Challenge Cup final: Catalans beat Warrington 20-14 to win first trophy|last=Scott|first=Ged|date=25 August 2018|work=BBC Sport|access-date=26 August 2018}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/aug/25/catalans-dragons-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final-match-report |title =Catalans Dragons beat Warrington in Challenge Cup final to make history|date=25 August 2018|work=Guardian|access-date=17 October 2018}}</ref>

During the [[Super League XXIV|2019 season]], Catalans Dragons held their home match against [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] at the [[Camp Nou]] in [[Barcelona]]. The match was the first [[Super League]] game in [[Spain]] and attracted a record attendance for a Super League game, outside of the [[Super League Grand Final|Grand Final]] or [[Magic Weekend]], of 31,555. The game resulted in a 33–16 victory for the Dragons.<ref>{{cite news | url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2019/may/18/catalan-dragons-wigan-warriors-super-league-match-report-camp-nou | title=Devastating Catalans prove too much for Wigan at enthusiastic Camp Nou | date=18 May 2019 | first=Aaron | last=Bower | newspaper=The Guardian | access-date=6 July 2020 }}</ref>

On 28 January 2020, Catalans Dragons announced that they had signed [[Israel Folau]] despite him holding to the Biblical teaching that those who practice homosexuality without repenting will go to hell (going so far as to suggest that the 2019–20 Australian bushfires were God's judgment on Australia for allowing same sex marriage and abortion).<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2019/nov/18/israel-folau-says-bushfires-and-drought-are-gods-punishment-for-same-sex-marriage-and-abortion|title=Israel Folau under fire for implying bushfires are God's punishment|date=18 November 2019|work=Guardian|access-date=29 January 2020}}</ref>
Wigan Warriors responded by saying that their next game against Catalans would be named Pride Day.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2020/jan/28/israel-folau-switches-codes-again-to-sign-for-catalans-dragons-rugby-league|title=Israel Folau: shock and outrage greets Catalans Dragons' signing|date=28 January 2020|work=Guardian|access-date=29 January 2020}}</ref>

In [[Super League XXVI|2021]], Catalans won Super League's [[League Leaders' Shield]] for the first time, following a 31–30 golden point extra time victory over reigning champions [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] during [[Magic Weekend]]. On 9 October 2021, Catalans played in their first ever [[Super League Grand Final]] with the opponents being reigning champions St Helens.  Catalans lead the match in the second half but a try late to St Helens saw Catalans lose 12–10.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/58858850|title=Super League Grand Final: Catalans Dragons 10-12 St Helens: Saints win three in a row|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>
In the 2022 Super League season, Catalans finished 4th on the table and qualified for the playoffs. The club would be eliminated from the playoffs in the first week, suffering a shock 20-10 loss against Leeds.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/62813693|title=Super League play-offs: Catalans Dragons 10-20 Leeds Rhinos - Liam Sutcliffe hat-trick secures win|work=BBC Sport }}</ref>

In the 2023 Super League season, Catalans finished second on the table, equal on points with Wigan who finished first. They would eventually reach the grand final after beating St Helens in the semi-final which ended the clubs four-year dominance of the competition. Catalans would go on to lose the [[2023 Super League Grand Final]] 10-2 against [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]. Catalans became the first team since the Super League era began in 1996 to not score a try in the final.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2023/oct/14/catalans-dragons-wigan-super-league-grand-final-rugby-league-match-report/|title=Wigan Warriors crowned Super League champions after downing Dragons|newspaper=The Observer|date=14 October 2023 |last1=Bower |first1=Aaron }}</ref>
In the [[2024 Super League season]], Catalans had a disappointing campaign finishing 7th on the table and missing out on the playoffs.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.superleague.co.uk/article/4681/2024-season-review-catalans-dragons#:~:text=In%20the%20first%20eight%20rounds,games%20in%20April%20and%20July./|title=Catalans Dragons 2024 season review|website=www.superleague.co.uk}}</ref>
In the 2025 Super League season, Catalans had an even tougher season than what they experienced in 2024 with the club finishing 9th on the table.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.totalrl.com/catalans-dragons-preparing-for-uncertain-future/|title=Catalans Dragons preparing for ‘uncertain future’|website=www.totalrl.com|accessdate=9 October 2025}}</ref>

==Stadiums==
===Stade Aimé Giral: 2006–2007===
{{Main|Stade Aimé Giral}}
The Catalans moved into the stadium in 2006, when they were accepted into [[Super League]]. They ground shared with rugby union side [[USA Perpignan]], which own and operate the stadium, but it is a multipurpose stadium used occasionally for other sports and events.

===Stade Gilbert Brutus: 2007–present===
[[File:Stade Gilbert Brutus 11112013.jpg|thumb|right|360px|Stade Gilbert Brutus]] 
{{Main|Stade Gilbert Brutus}}

The Stade Gilbert Brutus was opened in 1962 and has hosted mostly rugby league and union and hosted numerous test matches involving the [[France national rugby league team|France national team]]. It was previously the home to [[Northern Catalonia]] rugby league team. When the Dragons moved in the capacity was just over 4,000.

The Catalans have redeveloped and expanded the ground to 13,000 since they moved here.
